133651.explanation: The current plans are poorly thought-out and have an (too) large impact on livability, the environment, and traffic in our municipality. Citizens often know better than planners and policymakers where problems may arise. BVHlokaal supported the vision of the Citizen Coalition established for this purpose.

133652.explanation: No matter how sympathetic and important the goal is, this measure is unworkable. We would no longer be able to hire a small independent gardener if they still drive a petrol car. A good cause cannot always be turned into a strict rule. BVHlokaal stands for realistic policy.

133653.explanation: Residents are often better motivated (and cheaper) to maintain and improve greenery than a municipal department.

133654.explanation: It is important that residents have influence over where the money goes. This starts by electing the municipal council. They allocate the funds. Through policies and subsidies, this reaches the villages. For example, bike paths, events, playgrounds, extra greenery.

133655.explanation: Young people are often underrepresented in politics and representation, and this can help bring their interests and problems to attention.

133656.explanation: We see no immediate reason to increase the property tax (OZB) and do not want to burden our residents with costs without cause. And if possible, it may even be lowered. For example, in 2026, excess money collected from waste disposal fees was returned to the residents for the first time.

133657.explanation: A large part of our residents appreciate extended Sunday opening hours. Whether an entrepreneur opens or not is a free choice. We are therefore not in favor of mandatory opening or mandatory closing. That decision lies with the entrepreneurs themselves. We want to offer them the opportunity to open on Sundays.

133658.explanation: More nature-friendly agriculture is better for the environment in the long term (biodiversity, water, neighbors, and not least the farmer himself). The farmer deserves long-term clarity from The Hague to be able to invest and have time to make the transition.

133659.explanation: It is the (legal) responsibility of the national government and the province to provide funding. The municipality cannot bear this cost. However, the municipality must ensure that local regulations provide opportunities so that stopping or transitioning becomes attractive. An agricultural officer should assist farmers with this.

133660.explanation: More intensive livestock farming is not feasible. But we are positive about companies that want to farm more extensively/biologically in places where that is appropriate.

133661.explanation: If a field of solar panels can be integrated into the landscape without damaging the character of our villages, then that is possible for us.

133662.explanation: It is unlikely that a municipal energy company would be more efficient or cheaper than a commercial company.

133663.explanation: We cannot simply make these people homeless. That benefits no one, including surrounding residents.

133664.explanation: This is determined nationally. The municipality should not give more priority than is set at the national level.

133666.explanation: It is not the municipality's role to dictate how residents create atmosphere or heating in their homes. But every resident also has the right to a healthy living environment. We oppose wood burning that causes nuisance or risks. Therefore, we focus on knowledge sharing about how and when to burn properly.

133667.explanation: Tall wind turbines have a proven negative health effect on nearby residents. They should be placed in areas without residents or where residents are at a great distance. Our municipality does not have such locations.

133668.explanation: We believe the province should tackle problem wolves proactively. Capturing and relocating them (to areas with sufficient space, e.g. internationally) is an option if they come too close to small children or livestock.

133669.explanation: We think it is important that food is produced sustainably and we also want such entrepreneurs in our municipality.

133670.explanation: We oppose large national events that cause nuisance for many residents and the environment but benefit a (very) small number of entrepreneurs. We support local events that cause little to no nuisance for residents and nature and that fit our municipality (Flower Parade, castle tour, etc.).

133671.explanation: This area has great natural value, and the events for which permits have been requested cause too much and sometimes permanent damage and nuisance for neighbors.

133672.explanation: It makes the villages cozier, as it is a place for meeting. However, it must be in a location where residents and nature do not suffer nuisance.

133673.explanation: We oppose more or new permits for advertising in public spaces.

133674.explanation: Sports are important for health and the development of children. Sports must therefore be accessible to everyone.

133675.explanation: People nowadays move less than is good for them, and the municipality must encourage healthy movement as much as possible. This can also be done by walking more often or cycling.

133676.explanation: Artificial turf fields can be used much more than grass fields. Because we value physical activity and also encourage multifunctional use of sports fields (e.g., playgrounds for children, schools, or events), we prefer artificial turf over grass fields.

133677.explanation: Access to books and related services must be as low-threshold as possible, and distance plays an important role. BVHlokaal believes all villages should have such facilities and allocates funds accordingly.

133678.explanation: We support maintaining facilities in the villages.

133679.explanation: Play areas are important for the healthy development of children. These do not always have to be expensive play equipment. An open grassy field is often enough.

133680.explanation: Many sports clubs practise organized or unorganized sports on Sundays. The weekend has only two days. Such a measure would impede sports activities, whereas we want to encourage sports as much as possible. BVHlokaal considers Sunday sport a personal choice.

133681.explanation: The use of animals does not automatically mean animal suffering to us. Think of a petting zoo or a carriage ride. But this must never come at the expense of animal welfare. That is why the council has drafted an animal welfare policy. Animal suffering is never acceptable, with or without subsidies.

133682.explanation: There are neighborhoods with a lot of nuisance. To tackle that nuisance, camera surveillance is desirable. This is good for safety and preventing misconduct.

133683.explanation: The trade and use of drugs is an increasing problem in our municipality, so we must not enlarge it.

133684.explanation: The risk of forest fires is increasing. Good plans must be in place for prevention, suppression, and evacuation of people and animals.

133685.explanation: Community officers have a demonstrably positive role in recognizing, countering, and preventing crime. We also do not believe it is more expensive than the consequences of crime they help prevent.

133686.explanation: We follow national legislation on this point.

133687.explanation: Given the size of our municipality and an increased (feeling of) insecurity, we are in favor of more municipality enforcement officers and police on the streets.

133688.explanation: Municipal land is usually close to residential areas where hunting could pose a danger or nuisance to people and pets.

133691.explanation: Converting existing buildings is faster, cheaper, less environmentally burdensome, and less disruptive to neighbors than new construction. This should be project-based with professional developers and not at the municipality's expense and risk.

133692.explanation: We see a much greater demand in our municipality for mid-rent housing. Building according to the needs of our residents is more important to us than this principle.

133693.explanation: Nine floors leads to urbanization that too much undermines the village character of our municipality. Moreover, it is not necessary to meet the construction demand for 52,000 residents agreed upon by all parties.

133694.explanation: Unfortunately, we too often see (new) houses being bought up to be rented out at high prices. This makes new construction inaccessible for many of our residents. A purchaser clause for owner-occupation can prevent this.

133695.explanation: We oppose the establishment of companies that have (too) great an impact on traffic congestion or the living environment of our residents.

133696.explanation: We do not believe there is a great need for (even more) commercial space near Driebergen-Zeist station. We are certain there is a need for small-scale housing, so it seems logical to us to primarily build that.

133697.explanation: There are possibilities to convert a recreation park into a small neighborhood or community. The condition is that the infrastructure is suitable for this (roads, electricity, facilities such as education, medical care, etc.) and that no bottlenecks arise in our (sometimes scarce) facilities.

133698.explanation: Sometimes it is better to build on the land of a farmer who is stopping, or a small street along the edge of the village, than to fill every open space in the village centers.

133699.explanation: This number corresponds to the provincial and national agreements by which we meet the needs of our residents and the vitality of our villages. This way, we preserve the green spaces in the villages for playing, walking, and meeting.

133700.explanation: Sometimes it is better to build something on the land of a farmer who is stopping, or add a small street along the edge of the village, than to build on every open spot within the village centers.

133701.explanation: This is already the case. Only with two or more informal care dwellings is a permit application required.

133702.explanation: The municipality already offers various additional facilities and assistance for residents with a low income.

133703.explanation: We see this as desirable, but we do not believe in unconditional enforcement. There can be circumstances (e.g., children requiring intensive care or psychological problems) that make this unreasonable.

133704.explanation: This is already the case. And it must remain so. Everyone must be able to participate and contribute according to their ability.

133706.explanation: If people arrive somewhere by car or own one, they will also need to park it somewhere. Fewer parking spaces lead to more parking problems and nuisance for residents, causing irritation and worse. Only unused parking spaces could be converted into play areas or green spaces.

133707.explanation: Especially in places where children can play, and it is important that cars reduce their speed.

133708.explanation: Good public transport is an alternative to the car and one of the solutions for our traffic bottlenecks.

133709.explanation: This is a matter for our neighboring municipalities, not for us.

133711.explanation: There are already facilities for people with financial problems, and tailored solutions are almost always necessary.

133712.explanation: Healthcare is already under great pressure and is equal for everyone regardless of origin, religion, or orientation.

133713.explanation: Necessary care must be provided fully and properly. But we also see that some care is provided that could just as well or even better be given by the direct environment. We should be more selective about this.

133714.explanation: We do not quite see what role the municipality could or should have here. Support already exists for children who are victims of divorce. We do not see the municipality acting as a marriage counselor as desirable.

133651.explanation: A citizen's assembly can be useful as long as it enriches decision-making but does not replace it. Participation has taken place here, and the democratically elected municipal councils have already made a decision. Now we need to move forward with housing construction.

133652.explanation: The transition to sustainable energy is important and must be promoted. The energy transition should be pragmatic, focused on feasible, innovative, and affordable long-term solutions.

133653.explanation: We want a green and well-maintained municipality. Citizen initiatives should be given space and support from the municipality in the form of goods (such as plants) and use of tools.

133654.explanation: We support vital villages with sufficient facilities, such as sports, play, and meeting spaces. There should be enough subsidies for village activities to allow villages to maintain their own character.

133655.explanation: Young people must be involved in democracy. This can be through a youth council, representation in the municipal council, and participation.

133656.explanation: The VVD does not want an increase in property tax (OZB). We want to keep the burden on residents and entrepreneurs as low as possible.

133657.explanation: Residents should be able to decide when they want to do their shopping themselves.

133658.explanation: Nature-friendly agriculture must be encouraged. If livestock farmers want to switch, that must be made possible. We support nature management by farmers.

133659.explanation: It is good to help farmers convert their businesses to future-proof products (such as bio-based building materials and plant proteins). The municipality must cooperate to adjust regulations, but the province has received funding for this from the national government.

133660.explanation: We give farmers room to organize their businesses so they can run them innovatively and progressive. The condition is that this is possible from an environmental and nature perspective.

133661.explanation: The transition to sustainable energy is important, and we should not raise principled objections to it.

133662.explanation: The municipality can stimulate local initiatives. This can be through knowledge sharing or financial participation.

133663.explanation: The scarce space and resources we have must be spent on real refugees.

133664.explanation: The VVD chooses fair, sober, and realistic reception. But that reception must never come at the expense of Dutch people who have been waiting for a home for years. Alternative solutions must be found for people allowed to stay here and who also need a home.

133666.explanation: We assume that wood stove users handle their burning responsibly and take neighbors into account.

133667.explanation: The transition to sustainable energy is important, and we should not raise principled objections to it. There are hardly any locations in our municipality suitable for tall wind turbines.

133668.explanation: More control measures against wolves are necessary for the safety of people and animals.

133669.explanation: Initiatives for sustainable and local food are excellent. It is up to entrepreneurs to make the plans and to the municipality to enable this.

133670.explanation: We should cherish larger events such as the flower parade in Leersum, carnival in Driebergen, and the castle tour in Doorn. They provide conviviality, meeting opportunities, liveliness, and employment.

133671.explanation: Small-scale events may be limited as long as they do not have a negative impact on nature.

133672.explanation: Terraces provide conviviality and meeting places, as long as they do not cause nuisance to residents.

133673.explanation: Local retailers and organizations must be able to advertise. This also prevents illegal posting.

133674.explanation: Sports provide joy and relaxation. Therefore, we cooperate with schools and organizations to make sports possible for everyone.

133675.explanation: The municipality must ensure there are sufficient sports and play areas. Every resident should be able to be active.

133676.explanation: Artificial turf fields are less vulnerable to weather influences and result in fewer cancellations. For some sports, artificial turf fields are required by their sports association to participate in competitions.

133678.explanation: The municipality must ensure sufficient sports and playground facilities. Every resident should be able to be active. Being able to swim is vital in the Netherlands.

133679.explanation: We provide enough well-distributed places to play outside, and this can be done without higher taxes.

133680.explanation: The municipality must ensure sufficient sports and playground facilities. Every resident must have the freedom to be active every day of the week.

133681.explanation: The use of animals in an animal-friendly way must be possible. Think of the castle tour and the arrival of Sinterklaas.

133682.explanation: To make our municipality even safer, camera surveillance at strategic locations is useful.

133683.explanation: There are enough coffee shops in the municipality. Too many coffee shops cause nuisance and attract outsiders.

133684.explanation: Measures must be taken to reduce the risk of forest fires and their consequences.

133685.explanation: Neighborhood police officers are important for safety and preventing vandalism.

133686.explanation: Public service providers must be neutral.

133687.explanation: We tackle troublemakers with visible and invisible measures, such as deploying more enforcement officers (BOAs).

133688.explanation: Hunting is necessary for a healthy wildlife population. The province decides whether hunting is permitted or not.

133691.explanation: We facilitate the conversion of vacant offices and shops into housing through flexible regulations.

133692.explanation: We adhere to the national standard of 30% social and 40% mid-range housing. The demand for mid-range housing is the greatest.

133693.explanation: We want to preserve the village character.

133695.explanation: Small distribution centers for local entrepreneurs must be possible.

133696.explanation: The location near the station is ideal for housing close to public transport.

133697.explanation: Holiday homes should be used for their intended purpose. We want to maintain the policy that vacation homes are not used for permanent residence. Of course, the municipality remains in good consultation with involved residents to prevent distressing situations.

133698.explanation: We combine infill development in villages, if necessary from a vitality perspective, with expansions on the edges of the municipality, ensuring space for everyone and preserving sufficient greenery in our villages.

133699.explanation: There must be room for growth to keep our villages vital so that schools, shops and sports facilities can be maintained.

133700.explanation: We combine infill development in villages, if necessary from a vitality perspective, with expansions on the edges of the municipality, ensuring space for everyone and preserving sufficient greenery in our villages.

133701.explanation: We believe building a flexible home on one’s own property for caregiving purposes should be made easier.

133702.explanation: Work must always pay off. Anyone accepting a paid job from unemployment benefits should benefit financially.

133703.explanation: Everyone can participate in society. We ensure people contribute at their own level and pace, so they become part of social life again.

133704.explanation: Everyone can participate in society. We ensure people contribute at their own level and pace, so they become part of social life again.

133706.explanation: Sufficient parking spaces are important. Limiting the number of parking spaces should not mean residents in our expansive municipality can no longer park their car near their home or shops.

133707.explanation: These roads are generally not safer for cyclists and cause dangerous situations. It is better to construct good bicycle paths.

133708.explanation: We want to maintain connections between village centers and improved facilities and faster links to surrounding cities.

133709.explanation: That prevents truck traffic through Doorn and the National Park.

133711.explanation: The municipality must help residents and entrepreneurs get out of debt. Providing a stepping stone to people who need support for a while promotes self-reliance.

133712.explanation: Freedom of choice, including in healthcare, is very important to us.

133713.explanation: We support cost control and effective deployment of youth care.

133714.explanation: It is good to have support when needed. It should never be mandatory.

133651.explanation: D66 finds it important to involve house hunters and residents in housing plans; a citizens’ assembly can be a good tool for this. However, a regional vision for Driebergen-Zeist has already been decided upon, a great place to combine housing for youth/starters and public transport.

133652.explanation: D66 wants to stop using fossil fuels and promote green alternatives. But completely ceasing business with companies using fossil fuels is too extreme and currently not always feasible.

133653.explanation: D66 wants the municipality to actively help residents make their neighborhood greener, for example through subsidies, fewer rules, practical support or neighborhood budgets.

133654.explanation: A dedicated village budget is a way to give residents more control over their living environment. D66 would use that budget for projects like greenery, playgrounds, and small-scale infrastructure.

133655.explanation: D66 wants young people to have more say and decision-making power in politics and society. A youth council can help with this.

133656.explanation: D66 wants to keep housing costs affordable and not unnecessarily increase property tax (ozb). The property tax may only rise if truly necessary and clearly explained how the money will be spent.

133657.explanation: D66 supports freedom of choice and opportunities for entrepreneurs to also open on Sundays if they and their customers want that.

133658.explanation: D66 chooses more circular agriculture, food forests and nature development. We encourage livestock farmers’ choices in this direction.

133659.explanation: D66 supports healthy, sustainable farms. Farmers asking for help with conversion receive assistance, for example with permit applications, but we do not provide extra subsidies beyond national schemes.

133660.explanation: For D66, it is not logical to establish new intensive livestock farms in the municipality of Utrechtse Heuvelrug. However, D66 wants to support farmers who want to become more sustainable or switch practices.

133661.explanation: D66 wants to expand solar energy but is cautious about placing solar panels on agricultural land. Solar panels on land are only acceptable under conditions: there must be sufficient support and it must not come at the expense of nature.

133662.explanation: D66 is in favor of participation in a public heating company but wants the municipality to also approach the transition in cooperation with the market.

133663.explanation: D66 believes that the Netherlands should provide safe and good shelter to people fleeing war and violence. However, the municipality cannot be obligated to accommodate people whose asylum has been rejected.

133664.explanation: D66's position is that status holders are in a vulnerable position on the housing market and should therefore be given priority, just like other groups with a vulnerable position on the housing market.

133666.explanation: D66 is not in favor of a general ban on open fireplaces and wood stoves but wants to provide information so that people know when it is sensible not to burn, and offer cleaner alternatives.

133667.explanation: D66 views wind energy as part of the energy transition to meet climate goals, provided that landscape values, local residents, and local participation are taken into account.

133668.explanation: D66 emphasizes prevention, information, and protection of animals and people, not the standard catching, chasing away, or shooting of wolves. However, D66 supports shooting so-called problem wolves as a last resort and under strict conditions.

133669.explanation: D66 believes that governments have a (albeit limited) role here, such as informing residents, encouraging vegetable gardens, local food forests, better procurement by the municipality itself, etc.

133670.explanation: D66 wants to make events of all sizes possible, as long as there are good rules for safety, permits, and nuisance for local residents is limited.

133671.explanation: D66 wants nature value and tranquility to clearly come first in the future of the Henschotermeer, and that major disturbances caused by events are limited. Small-scale activities in nature during daylight hours fit within this.

133672.explanation: D66 supports initiatives to give cafes and restaurants more space for terraces, emphasizing cooperation with entrepreneurs, customization, and clear frameworks.

133673.explanation: D66 does not want to stop all advertising permits in public spaces but wants the municipality to consider which advertisements are appropriate. Outdoor advertising can sometimes clutter the streetscape and obstruct traffic views, which is bad for traffic safety.

133674.explanation: D66 wants to make sports accessible to everyone. Financial barriers can be reduced, for example, by using funds like the Youth Sports Fund and deploying neighborhood sports coaches so that more children and adults can participate in sports.

133675.explanation: D66 believes the municipality has a role in promoting sports and physical activity for all groups, including young people, elderly, and vulnerable groups, and that this should be supported with policies and programs to make movement easier and more accessible.

133676.explanation: D66 wants sustainable, high-quality, and healthy sports facilities and believes the municipality should decide on the type of field in consultation with the relevant sports association.

133677.explanation: D66 wants residents to have easy access to a library because libraries encourage reading, learning, language development, and building digital skills.

133678.explanation: D66 supports future-proof and good sports facilities. This can mean consolidating (outdated) facilities into one new, modern facility in certain cases. Woestduin and De Zwoer are outdated and require attention.

133679.explanation: D66 actively works to preserve playgrounds and wants structural increases in funding for maintenance and renewal of (accessible) playgrounds so all children can play safely outside.

133680.explanation: D66 wants to support sports clubs and advocates for a broad sports offer regardless of the day of the week.

133681.explanation: D66 does not hold the position that cultural events must always and necessarily be free of animals, but supports a broader view on animal welfare that may lead to a critical assessment of the use of animals at events.

133682.explanation: Camera surveillance is a police tool used in places where situations get out of hand. D66 believes the world does not automatically become safer with more cameras. Privacy and freedom of residents are important considerations.

133683.explanation: D66's position on coffee shop policy focuses mainly on creating safe, controlled conditions and minimizing street dealing as much as possible.

133684.explanation: D66 values animal and nature protection. Forest fires in Utrechtse Heuvelrug are a real risk. Plans to protect animals during forest fires should therefore be included in broader disaster plans.

133685.explanation: D66 wants sufficient police capacity in all our villages so that neighborhood officers have the time and space to fulfill their role locally. We do not see the need for a fixed standard to be prescribed.

133686.explanation: D66 does not focus on appearances. It is important that enforcement officers are suitable for the role, behave professionally, and are service-oriented.

133687.explanation: D66 does not necessarily want more enforcement officers but wants enforcement officers, police, and other partners to ensure safe and livable neighborhoods through good task division, cooperation, and preventive efforts.

133688.explanation: D66 supports strict regulations on hunting (no hunting of protected species). D66 wants hunting linked to nature interests and damage control.

133691.explanation: D66 strongly supports this. We see it as an opportunity to create housing more quickly for people who currently have difficulty finding a home.

133692.explanation: D66 finds affordable housing important and wants 30% of total homes to consist of social rental housing. Catching up is necessary, also for the middle segment.

133693.explanation: D66 advocates compact building in vulnerable areas. That means more green space between buildings and sometimes slightly taller buildings, but not routinely allowing 9 storeys high.

133694.explanation: You buy a house to live in it yourself. That is D66's starting point. This is good for home ownership and livability in villages and neighborhoods. That is why D66 recently initiated purchase protection.

133695.explanation: D66 does not want the municipality to allow the construction of distribution centers. Space is limited: nature, housing, work, and accessibility must be balanced, and unwanted cluttering of the landscape must be prevented.

133696.explanation: D66 supports small-scale housing for young and old. The station area is a regional gateway where nature, housing, and mobility come together in a lively neighborhood.

133697.explanation: D66 believes it should be permitted for holiday parks to allow permanent residence in some of their holiday homes. However, we maintain that holiday parks are intended for vacationers.

133698.explanation: D66 believes new space for housing development should be sought if necessary to solve the housing shortage. However, careful consideration must be given to where and how construction takes place, with attention to nature and the living environment.

133699.explanation: D66 wants to speed up and stimulate housing construction, focusing on diversity in housing supply and smart use of existing space. No building freeze.

133700.explanation: D66 is open to housing development on agricultural land if needed. This must be carefully weighed with attention to the environment, landscape, and living environment.

133701.explanation: D66 believes that the municipality should not create unnecessary obstacles when placing caregiving homes.

133702.explanation: D66 believes income support should align with what people truly need. This means customization must remain possible. D66 finds this more important than raising the current threshold, which is common among medium-sized municipalities.

133703.explanation: D66 wants to encourage participation and support people towards (paid) work or other meaningful activities, but not through coercion or obligations. It should not be a strict condition to retain a social assistance benefit.

133704.explanation: D66 believes the municipality should actively contribute to helping people with a distance to the labor market find work. This can be done through subsidized jobs, but there are other possibilities as well. These resources should be used as a stepping stone to regular employment.

133706.explanation: Reducing the number of parking spaces when the number of homes increases is illogical. D66 does want more car-reduced neighborhoods to be built and encourages car sharing.

133707.explanation: D66 supports the idea of more streets where cars are 'guests', with designs that prioritize cyclists and pedestrians. This fits within our vision of a sustainable, safe, and livable public space.

133708.explanation: D66 wants improvements in public transport, including a direct bus connection between Leersum and Amersfoort Central Station.

133709.explanation: The N229 lies outside the municipality of Utrechtse Heuvelrug. We do not have authority over it. However, D66 wants to limit transit truck traffic through all our villages. The municipality is discussing this with neighboring municipalities and the province.

133711.explanation: D66 believes the municipality should not lend money to residents casually. What D66 does want is for debt assistance to be organized more humanely, effectively, and quickly. Debt restructuring credits could be part of this.

133712.explanation: D66 wants everyone to receive appropriate care and for preferences to be seriously considered in care choices so people can choose a care provider who suits them. But this must be within the possibilities of the care system, so it’s not always possible.

133713.explanation: D66 believes children and young people who need youth care should have access to the right care at the right time. This also means youth care should not be too heavy if lighter support is sufficient.

133714.explanation: D66 wants children and adults to be safe, including at home. People who need help with this will receive it, regardless of family composition.

133651.explanation: The CDA supports a citizens' assembly for the Driebergen-Zeist station building plan. This offers opportunities to tackle the housing shortage while respecting our valuable nature. Together with residents, we create a supported vision for a vital and green living environment.

133652.explanation: The CDA chooses a sustainable future and wants the municipality to set a good example itself. We want to collaborate with companies that take responsibility for climate and nature. However, it must be realistic, without losing sight of support and the results of measures.

133653.explanation: The CDA wholeheartedly supports this. Green spaces strengthen our biodiversity and provide cooling in neighborhoods. We embrace positive initiatives from residents that improve livability. Together we make our villages more beautiful and invest in a healthy, vital living environment.

133654.explanation: The CDA believes in the strength of our residents. They know best what their village and neighborhood need. Their own budget gives room to their own strength and responsibility, strengthens mutual bonds, and ensures governance that truly listens to the people.

133655.explanation: The CDA believes in the voice of our youth. Their ideas and energy are indispensable for future-proof policies. A youth council gives them space to think along and participate. Together we build villages where everyone feels heard.

133656.explanation: The municipality had a surplus of over 40 million euros in the past five years. Therefore, the CDA finds further increases of the property tax (OZB) unnecessary and unfair. We want to freeze the charges and even return future surpluses to residents.

133657.explanation: The CDA values the common day of rest but respects the freedom of entrepreneurs and residents. We want to keep current rules: shops may open on Sundays provided staff are not forced. This way rest and freedom of choice go hand in hand in our villages.

133658.explanation: The CDA supports our farmers in the transition to a sustainable future. By promoting nature-friendly farming, we protect our biodiversity and strengthen the landscape on both sides of the hills. Together we build a vital countryside where farming and nature go hand in hand.

133659.explanation: The CDA sees no reason to pay farmers to stop their business. This is a national government matter. Additionally, farms and stables can be replaced by homes, which generates a lot of money and can help solve the housing shortage.

133660.explanation: The CDA sees no need or room for new livestock farms but chooses balance. We support existing agricultural businesses that work responsibly and care for nature and landscape. Together we build a sustainable future where agriculture and nature reinforce each other, not burden.

133661.explanation: The CDA wants solar panels first on roofs. However, we do not exclude farmland for smart combinations, such as panels under which sheep can graze. This way we generate energy while keeping the land usable for farmers and preserving our beautiful landscape.

133662.explanation: The CDA considers its own energy company too risky and expensive. The municipality should not take risks with taxpayers' money and does not have this expertise in-house. We prefer to support local sustainable initiatives from residents; that ensures sustainability in our own villages.

133663.explanation: Those who have exhausted their appeals must return to their country of origin. We focus on refugees who are legally allowed to stay here. For them, we have the asylum seekers' center in Leersum, the accommodation of Ukrainians in Doorn, and status holders who have found a home in our municipality.

133664.explanation: The CDA opposes automatic priority for status holders. The municipality should decide who the most urgent and vulnerable housing seekers are. We want a fair distribution where a person's personal situation is decisive.

133666.explanation: The CDA chooses realistic climate policies with support, not coercive bans. A fireplace represents warmth and coziness, provided it is used responsibly for clean air. We prefer to make sustainable improvements through insulation and preserving personal freedom of choice.

133667.explanation: The CDA wants to protect our green Heuvelrug. High wind turbines harm the landscape and livability. We choose sustainable energy with support and respect for nature and village character. Together we seek solutions that truly fit our environment.

133668.explanation: The CDA chooses safety and well-being for our residents and their livestock. The wolf causes great unrest among our residents and damage to entrepreneurs. We want decisive governance that takes concerns seriously and protects our community.

133669.explanation: The CDA fully supports this. By making local food accessible, we strengthen our farmers and shorten the chain. This promotes a healthy lifestyle and realistic climate policy. Together we invest in a vital economy and a fair, green future.

133670.explanation: The CDA cherishes our traditions. A ban on larger events would mean the end for major attractions like the Flower Parade in Leersum or the Grietmarkt in Amerongen. We want room for both small neighborhood parties and these defining events that connect our villages.

133671.explanation: The CDA wants to prevent disturbance from events to residents. We choose strict agreements about traffic and noise so events do not disrupt peace in our villages. Coordination with the municipality of Woudenberg is important because Henschotermeer is largely in their territory.

133672.explanation: Terraces bring liveliness and strengthen social cohesion in our villages. We grant entrepreneurs space, provided good agreements are made to limit nuisance for residents. Together we maintain the balance between conviviality and peaceful living.

133673.explanation: A total ban harms local entrepreneurs and associations who want to publicize their activities. The CDA wants no uncontrolled proliferation of advertisements but does want space for local businesses and announcements of village activities. We choose clear rules instead of a general ban.

133674.explanation: The CDA believes every child and adult should be able to play sports regardless of their financial situation. Sport is healthy, prevents loneliness, and brings people together in our villages. By paying membership fees for low-income residents, we ensure everyone can participate and no one is excluded.

133675.explanation: The CDA finds movement essential for everyone. Targeted programs help prevent loneliness among the elderly and promote a healthy lifestyle among young people. This way we ensure vulnerable groups remain vital and can fully participate in our villages.

133676.explanation: The CDA opposes mandatory replacement (who will pay for that?). For sports like hockey and korfball, artificial turf is a strict requirement of the sports associations for competitive matches. Moreover, artificial turf is necessary for the high training intensity of our sports clubs.

133677.explanation: The CDA believes every village deserves a library. The library is an essential place for reading pleasure, language skills, and digital skills. As a meeting place for young and old, it strengthens the community. This way we keep facilities and personal development accessible in our villages.

133678.explanation: The CDA wants to keep both swimming pools. Local facilities are essential for livability in our villages. One central swimming pool is not realistic (and who will pay for that?). We choose to maintain what we have.

133679.explanation: The CDA wants to retain the main central playgrounds. If other playgrounds are also heavily used, the municipality can help maintain the playground together with the neighborhood. The municipality has had millions of euros surplus in recent years; higher taxes are therefore not needed.

133680.explanation: The CDA believes sport must remain accessible to everyone. Subsidies are intended for social impact and health. We respect Sunday rest but leave the choice of sports times to the clubs and athletes themselves.

133681.explanation: The CDA finds a total ban unnecessary. Animals are part of our culture and education, such as at petting zoos, sheepfolds, or the arrival of Sinterklaas. As long as all animal welfare rules are met, the use of animals by subsidized organizations should remain possible.

133682.explanation: The CDA unconditionally prioritizes the safety of our residents. Camera surveillance at hotspots acts as a deterrent and helps address nuisance and crime. We want everyone in our villages to always feel safe; safety is the foundation of our society.

133683.explanation: The CDA advocates for the protection of our youth and public health. One coffee shop in the municipality is sufficient. New locations increase the risks of nuisance and crime. We want a safe, healthy environment where trusted coexistence and prevention are top priorities.

133684.explanation: The CDA believes we must, as good stewards, care for our nature and the animals living there. A rescue plan for humans and animals in case of forest fires is essential to protect us. Safety and care for creation go hand in hand.

133685.explanation: The CDA calls for a visible and approachable police presence in our villages. The community officer is crucial for residents' sense of security. The community officer knows the neighborhood, detects problems early, and builds a relationship with people. Together, this is how we invest in a safe society.

133686.explanation: The CDA values a neutral and uniform appearance of enforcement officers acting on behalf of the government. A uniform without religious or ideological symbols contributes to the recognizability and objectivity of the public order officers. Residents must be able to trust that these officers act neutrally.

133687.explanation: The CDA advocates for more enforcement officers (public order officers) on the streets to safeguard the livability of our villages. They are our eyes and ears in the neighborhood and take action against nuisance. More officers provide a more direct point of contact and strengthen residents' sense of security.

133688.explanation: The CDA chooses professional and responsible nature management. Hunting is only allowed when population management is essential for traffic safety and preventing damage to valuable nature. We aim for a healthy balance between people, animals, and landscape in our beautiful municipality.

133691.explanation: The CDA supports transforming vacant offices and commercial buildings into homes. This is a smart way to address the housing shortage without sacrificing our precious green spaces. By promoting reuse, we quickly create new housing for starters and seniors.

133692.explanation: The housing shortage is too severe to delay projects. The CDA especially wants more new homes for our own starters and elderly, including social housing. We choose tailored approaches per project, ensuring plans are actually implemented and homes become available faster.

133693.explanation: The CDA wants to build with respect for the green character of our villages. Nine-story buildings do not fit the human scale and identity of the Heuvelrug. We opt for creative solutions within current height regulations to tackle the housing shortage without urbanizing our villages.

133694.explanation: The CDA supports the introduction of owner-occupancy requirements. Homes are meant to be lived in, not as investment objects for speculators. By requiring buyers to live in their homes, we give starters and families a fair chance at affordable housing in their own environment.

133695.explanation: The Utrechtse Heuvelrug is not a place for large-scale distribution centers. These 'box-like' buildings damage our valuable landscape and cause significant traffic pressure on our village roads. The CDA favors small-scale local businesses that suit the character of our green municipality.

133696.explanation: The CDA wants to build many more homes and sees the station area as an excellent location for this. We want more affordable homes instead of offices. We want an attractive, lively area with modern apartments for young and old – not cold, ugly apartment blocks.

133697.explanation: The CDA supports all realistic options to solve the housing shortage. Permanent residence may be allowed on a few small sites. Additionally, other sites can be used for residents in need, such as after a divorce, but only under conditions and in consultation with the social village team.

133698.explanation: The CDA supports new construction on the edges of our villages to truly address the housing shortage. Filling all the empty spots in our villages is not a solution, quite the contrary. The province must provide space for building beyond current boundaries. This way, we create the urgently needed homes.

133699.explanation: The CDA opposes a hard building stop. A predetermined population limit should not block the urgently needed homes for our own starters and elderly. We choose controlled growth, where the quality of our landscape and the livability of the villages always take precedence.

133700.explanation: The CDA favors new construction on agricultural land to truly tackle the housing shortage. Filling all the empty spots in our villages is no solution, quite the opposite. By carefully building outside the built-up area, we give our own residents renewed hope for a home.

133701.explanation: The CDA wants to fully support caregivers. Permit-free placement of caregiver homes makes care close to home possible faster. This encourages residents to care for each other in their familiar environment, which directly relieves pressure on professional care.

133702.explanation: The CDA believes that no one in our municipality should fall between the cracks. By raising the income limit, we also support working poor and families just above the minimum income who struggle to make ends meet. Solidarity is our guiding principle.

133703.explanation: The CDA believes in a society where everyone contributes according to their ability. Meaningful social work strengthens self-worth, keeps people connected to the community, and is also a valuable step toward paid employment. Together, this builds a vital and inclusive municipality.

133704.explanation: The CDA believes everyone deserves a place in the labor market. A subsidized job is a powerful means to offer people with disabilities or distance from work a new perspective. This stimulates social participation, increases self-reliance, and utilizes everyone’s talents.

133706.explanation: The CDA opposes the removal of parking spaces. For livability and the local economy, our villages must remain easily accessible, also for elderly and visitors from outside the village. Fewer parking spaces lead to more search traffic and nuisance in residential areas.

133707.explanation: The CDA supports the creation of more bicycle streets where cars are guests. This increases traffic safety, especially on routes to schools and sports clubs. This encourages healthy movement and makes our villages more livable, while respecting the necessary accessibility for destination traffic.

133708.explanation: The CDA finds good regional accessibility essential for our residents. A direct bus connection between Leersum and Amersfoort Central Station significantly reduces travel time. This makes public transport a fully-fledged alternative to the car and strengthens the link between our villages and the city.

133709.explanation: The CDA supports opening the bus lane on the N229 to freight traffic from Wijk bij Duurstede to the A12 in Bunnik. This prevents trucks from leaving the A12 near Maarsbergen and driving via the Donderberg roundabout in Leersum and the intersection in Doorn to Wijk bij Duurstede.

133711.explanation: The CDA wants to help residents with heavy debts start anew with a clean slate. With a municipal loan, they can pay off their debts, giving the family peace of mind and stopping further costs from accumulating. They will have to repay the loan eventually, but there is time for that.

133712.explanation: The CDA considers good and timely care for everyone most important. We focus on respectful care, where the human dimension and quality always form the basis.

133713.explanation: For the CDA, every child counts. Stricter rules often lead to more bureaucracy and delays, while vulnerable children need quick and appropriate help. We do not want more rules, but better cooperation between the municipality and organizations for the right support.

133714.explanation: The municipality cannot prevent divorces, but it can provide guidance in problematic high-conflict divorces involving children. To prevent trauma and prolonged suffering for those involved, the municipality can offer assistance.

133651.explanation: A citizen's assembly ensures broad resident involvement in important decisions about the area vision. By lottery, people who normally do not participate also get a voice, leading to better-supported decisions.

133652.explanation: The municipality must set a good example. Collaborating with fossil fuel companies perpetuates pollution and conflicts with climate goals and climate neutrality.

133653.explanation: Green neighborhoods provide more biodiversity, less heat, and better health. Resident initiatives deserve support because they know what is needed locally.

133654.explanation: Villages differ greatly from each other. Having their own budget increases participation, involvement, and tailor-made solutions, strengthening local democracy.

133655.explanation: Young people are strongly affected by decisions about climate, housing, and nature. A youth council gives them structural influence and strengthens democracy.

133656.explanation: A higher property tax may be necessary for investments in nature, climate, and care, but must not disproportionately affect lower incomes. Differentiation and compensation are important.

133657.explanation: Peace and quiet are important, but municipalities must allow room for local considerations. Protection of workers weighs more heavily than commercial interests.

133658.explanation: Transitioning to nature-friendly and plant-based agriculture is necessary for animal welfare, nature restoration, and public health.

133659.explanation: Farmers who wish to stop or convert must be fairly supported. This prevents vacancies, debts, and creates space for nature and housing.

133660.explanation: New livestock farms cause additional animal suffering, nitrogen emissions, and health risks for residents nearby. The sector should shrink instead.

133661.explanation: Agricultural land is needed for food production and nature. Solar panels should primarily be placed on roofs, facades, parking lots, and infrastructure.

133662.explanation: A municipal energy company keeps energy affordable, sustainable, and publicly owned, without profit motives or fossil interests.

133663.explanation: People without residency status also have the right to humane shelter. This prevents distressing situations and is a matter of civilization.

133664.explanation: The housing shortage is the problem, not the refugee. Priority increases polarization. The solution lies in more social housing.

133666.explanation: Wood burning causes severe air pollution and health problems. A ban protects vulnerable residents and improves air quality.

133667.explanation: Wind energy is necessary for a sustainable future. The municipality should not fundamentally oppose tall wind turbines but should guide careful location choices, outside nature areas and with protection for people and animals.

133668.explanation: The wolf belongs to nature and contributes to biodiversity. Capturing or chasing it away is not a solution. The focus should be on good education and prevention, such as protecting livestock.

133669.explanation: Local and sustainable food is better for the climate, nature, and health. The municipality can stimulate this through short supply chains, local markets, and support for plant-based agriculture.

133670.explanation: Small-scale events foster connection without major pressure on nature, the living environment, and animals. Large-scale events do not suit a vulnerable natural area.

133671.explanation: Henschotermeer is located in and near vulnerable nature. Events cause disturbance, litter, and stress for animals. Peace and nature must come first here.

133672.explanation: Terraces contribute to liveliness but must not come at the expense of peace, nature, accessibility, and residents. Tailored solutions per location are needed.

133673.explanation: Advertising in public spaces pollutes the streetscape and leads to more consumption. Less advertising means more peace, safety, and space for greenery.

133674.explanation: Sports are important for health and social cohesion. Money should not be a barrier. The municipality must make sports accessible to everyone.

133675.explanation: Targeted exercise programs improve health, reduce healthcare costs, and increase well-being, especially among youth, elderly, and vulnerable groups.

133676.explanation: Artificial turf contains microplastics and is harmful to the environment and health. Real grass is more sustainable, cooler, and better for nature and biodiversity.

133677.explanation: Libraries are essential for meeting, education, and digital skills. Every village deserves an accessible library function.

133678.explanation: Swimming pools are important, but accessibility, sustainability, and costs must be carefully weighed. One new swimming pool allows room for nature and housing.

133679.explanation: Playgrounds are important for children, health, and social contacts. They contribute to livable neighborhoods and must not disappear due to budget cuts.

133680.explanation: Sports must remain accessible to everyone. The municipality should not focus on ideology but allow freedom of choice.

133681.explanation: Animals are not for entertainment. Institutions receiving public subsidies must operate in an animal- and nature-friendly way and not use animals.

133682.explanation: Camera surveillance can sometimes help but is not a cure-all and affects privacy. Prevention, social cohesion, and good lighting are often more effective.

133683.explanation: The focus is mainly on health, safety, and livability. The municipality must be able to regulate location and number.

133684.explanation: During forest fires, (wild) animals are at great risk. A rescue plan is necessary and should be part of disaster plans, with attention to animal welfare.

133685.explanation: A visible community police officer increases safety, trust, and prevention. The municipality must actively urge the police on this.

133686.explanation: Freedom of religion and inclusivity are important. As long as enforcement remains neutral and professional, municipal enforcement officers should be allowed to wear religious expressions.

133687.explanation: More municipal enforcement officers ensure better enforcement, livability, and safety, especially regarding nuisance, traffic, and nature protection.

133688.explanation: Hunting causes animal suffering and disrupts nature. Hunting must be prohibited on municipal land; nature must be left alone.

133691.explanation: Repurposing saves space, prevents vacancy, and is more sustainable than new construction in green areas.

133692.explanation: 40% social rental housing is necessary to keep living affordable for low and middle incomes.

133693.explanation: High-rise buildings are possible but must fit the landscape, nature, and village scale. Livability takes precedence over height.

133694.explanation: Homes are for living in, not for speculation. Owner-occupancy requirements prevent buyouts by investors.

133695.explanation: Distribution centers cause a lot of traffic, emissions, and landscape damage, while providing few local jobs.

133696.explanation: Small-scale housing fits better with the environment and reduces pressure on nature and infrastructure.

133697.explanation: We do not want housing in the rural area. Permanent residence is not a solution and will lead to new recreational homes elsewhere. The solution to the housing problem lies in more social housing.

133698.explanation: Building outside village boundaries harms nature and landscape. First, build within existing areas and use vacancies.

133699.explanation: Population size is not leading, but the carrying capacity of nature, infrastructure, and facilities.

133700.explanation: Agricultural land is needed for food and nature. Building is only allowed if the livestock population is reduced proportionally.

133701.explanation: Informal caregiving must be accessible. Temporary informal care homes should not be unnecessarily bureaucratic.

133702.explanation: Many people currently fall just outside the schemes. A higher income limit prevents debt and stress.

133703.explanation: Mandatory counter-performances often feel humiliating and do not help people sustainably into work.

133704.explanation: Subsidized jobs give people perspective, income, and dignity and prevent long-term dropout.

133706.explanation: Fewer parking spaces means less car traffic and more space for greenery, cycling, and social interaction.

133707.explanation: This makes streets safer, quieter, and more pleasant, providing space for pedestrians and cyclists.

133708.explanation: Good public transport connections are crucial for accessibility, livability, and reducing car traffic.

133709.explanation: The bus lane is intended for fast and reliable public transport. Freight traffic causes delays and safety issues.

133711.explanation: A debt relief credit provides peace and clarity and helps people become debt-free faster.

133712.explanation: People should receive care that suits them. This increases trust and the effectiveness of care.

133713.explanation: Stricter rules lead to delays in help. Children should be helped quickly and with low barriers.

133714.explanation: Preventive support can prevent much suffering, especially for children, and reduces future care costs.

133651.explanation: The council is elected once every four years and has the mandate to make decisions. In cases of consultation to gather ideas, this can already be done with the current participation policy.

133652.explanation: As long as there are not enough alternatives available, we exclude nothing.

133653.explanation: Subsidy to (partially) contribute to the costs.

133654.explanation: We are one municipality. There are all kinds of subsidies to apply for, where every village is treated equally. Providing structural funding for unspecified matters creates unnecessary separate funds.

133655.explanation: It is not the municipality's role to organize this. It must come from the youth themselves. In the past, it has been shown that only then are enough people willing to commit to it.

133656.explanation: The overall tax burden must go down, so increasing the property tax (OZB) is not obvious.

133657.explanation: Sunday is the day every person should spend in service to God. If you do not want to do that, it is always good to have a day of rest and not maintain a 24/7 economy.

133658.explanation: The province sets the nature targets and the sector decides how to achieve them. The municipality can facilitate this.

133659.explanation: If there are forced terminations, the municipality must have a financial safety net for this group. This can prevent premature (preventive) reliance on various facilities.

133660.explanation: We do not close everything in advance. Where possible, within the policy, new business operations are allowed.

133661.explanation: Agricultural land is scarce and should be used for agriculture and food production, not as building land for solar fields. There are still sufficient alternative locations, such as industrial roofs and along highways.

133662.explanation: The municipality is too small for this. This requires major investments to get the infrastructure in order, which fits better with a single heat company for the entire province.

133663.explanation: The municipality cooperates in the deportation of those who stay illegally in the Netherlands and whose procedures have been concluded. Temporary shelter can only be an option in emergency situations.

133664.explanation: This concerns status holders. Every resident of our municipality has equal right to housing. A status holder can use the same urgency rules as everyone else if the situation requires it.

133666.explanation: Facilitate good advice and a good heating guide. Certain homes allow only wood burning; you cannot leave them in the cold. Investments and adjustments are costly.

133667.explanation: High wind turbines are not profitable in our low-wind municipality. We only allow so-called farm windmills (low).

133668.explanation: The options to address nuisance must be expanded. This can result in capturing, relocating, or even shooting.

133669.explanation: Regulations, for example to sell local food products (at home), must be relaxed.

133670.explanation: If there are any events, they fit our municipality and cause no nuisance to the environment and surroundings. Naturally, Sunday rest is maintained.

133671.explanation: Nature reserves are not locations for events.

133672.explanation: This mostly comes at the expense of outdoor space and parking places. The outdoor space will look messier with more terraces.

133673.explanation: All offensive, unchristian advertising must be banned. Other advertising should remain possible.

133674.explanation: There are subsidies and other possibilities. Sports are not a core task of the municipality. (Sports) clubs must take their own social responsibility.

133675.explanation: There must be attention for ALL residents. There is already policy for youths (on healthy weight, etc.). The municipality does not create programs itself but stimulates this, such as neighborhood sports coaches.

133676.explanation: No further (unnecessary) investments in sports. We maintain what we have and do not expand; we replace only when truly necessary. Maintaining natural grass requires ongoing investments and more space.

133677.explanation: Small villages are not large enough to have their own facilities. We want to maintain the quality of the current offerings.

133678.explanation: These villages are within accessible distance. The number of swimming pools in our municipality is very high compared to the population and not financially sustainable. When pools need replacement, one new pool will be built/merged.

133679.explanation: There is a playground policy providing the necessary locations. Expansion is not desired. At most, make replacements more inclusive, where every child can (join) play.

133680.explanation: Sunday is the day every person should spend in service to God. Sports does not belong there. Facilities can be used six days a week.

133681.explanation: Every subsidy application is assessed. Depending on the cultural aspect, animals may be allowed.

133682.explanation: If the situation demands it, in consultation with Justice, police, and Mayor, this is permitted.

133683.explanation: Although it meets a certain need, there is often nuisance around these premises, apart from what users do to buy or be able to buy. The existing coffeeshop (in Driebergen) must therefore also close.

133684.explanation: This is not a basic duty of the municipality. In case of emergencies, this is managed properly by, for example, the fire department. The position of the human being comes first.

133685.explanation: The municipality follows the nationally recommended guideline in this respect.

133686.explanation: Enforcers are allowed to wear a form of religious expression.

133687.explanation: More police presence on the streets is otherwise not feasible, so more BOA's (municipal enforcement officers) are necessary. However, the national government must provide funding for this. The municipality cannot continue to finance this on its own.

133688.explanation: The term hunting includes much more than just hunting animals. Catching pests also falls under hunting. Managing animals is necessary and sometimes unavoidable shoot-downs occur.

133691.explanation: Vacancy is not good but we combat it in other ways. These buildings are not owned by the municipality, so it has no say over them. The municipality is not a real estate company.

133692.explanation: Disagree. In neighborhoods that already have a lot of social housing, you actually want to add other segments to create mixed neighborhoods.

133693.explanation: High-rise buildings on the edges of the centers, in stepped increasing construction, can offer a solution to the housing shortage without unnecessary land use. This preserves more of the green character of the municipality.

133694.explanation: Investors and slumlords should not be encouraged. We enforce the current owner-occupancy requirement as it is leading in most cases.

133695.explanation: These do not fit in our (rural) area and require a lot of space. Additionally, they place a heavy burden on the energy supplies.

133696.explanation: This location is especially suitable for this type of construction, certainly considering the existing buildings. However, high-rise buildings can also contain multiple small-scale housing units.

133697.explanation: Recreation is recreation, not permanent residence, and is urgently needed to meet recreational demand. Otherwise, owners are unjustly rewarded with financial benefits (upon sale) and inequality arises by suddenly allowing living in nature here.

133698.explanation: There are edge-of-core zones that can be considered, especially in the smaller centers.

133699.explanation: Not feasible, what must happen is that facilities remain matched to the numbers and we continue building according to demand.

133700.explanation: Agricultural land is primarily for food production and livestock farming. In the edge-of-core zones, we use space smartly. Living on agricultural land is only an option if high-rise in the village or edge-of-core construction is undesirable and there remains high demand for homes in the respective village.

133701.explanation: Current policy already allows this and should remain so. However, supervision is needed to ensure it is not a mess, but of quality.

133702.explanation: The policy in our municipality is already above average and very generous.

133703.explanation: Reciprocity, where possible, is Biblical. Doing nothing and holding out your hand is not an option.

133704.explanation: Not paying, there are other means and agencies for that. In any case, facilitating as much as possible.

133706.explanation: Actually expanding at some locations. Reorganizing is also an option. Especially around nature reserves.

133707.explanation: No 'car as guest' experiments. Streets where the speed limit is or becomes 30 km/h are designed safely, appropriate to the traffic volumes in that street.

133708.explanation: A solid and frequent connection does not exist at all now and is highly desirable.

133709.explanation: Not a task for our municipality. We have no say in this and cannot change it.

133711.explanation: There is already much policy on debt reduction. Credit only makes spending 'on credit' easier and increases the problem. It also does not encourage lifestyle adjustment.

133712.explanation: The Personal Budget (PGB) offers a good solution here.

133713.explanation: No stacking of care, because there is already care. (it must not become a status symbol to have care) Furthermore, the care must be monitored to see if the care provided helps. If there is no improvement, stop this care. Harmful and pointless therapies (autism cure) are not allowed.

133714.explanation: A divorce desk already exists. The municipality must focus especially on prevention, so that preventive actions can be taken (relationship help) and (costs in) youth care do not grow further. Without the children becoming victims as a result of the divorce.

133651.explanation: Spatial projects must always be accompanied by good participation. This applies to initiatives from both the municipality and companies and individuals. Decision-making takes place in local democracy (the municipal council). A citizen council is a system beside a system.

133652.explanation: We would like that, but our society is not there yet.

133653.explanation: This can only work if accompanied by a transparent accountability system.

133654.explanation: The municipal council is responsible for the budgets and ensures that all villages, respecting their uniqueness, are treated equally. Village centers get more autonomy to make choices that suit them, but this requires a residents’ platform.

133655.explanation: Young people cannot vote yet, but they do have ideas. And it often concerns their future.

133656.explanation: For the Christian Union it is reasonable and necessary that property tax (OZB) rises with inflation. After all, all costs rise, so why should the OZB not be adjusted for inflation? The OZB is an important source of income for the municipality to maintain our provisions.

133657.explanation: In principle, we want Sunday rest because it is good for society. The current practice is acceptable, given the differing opinions on this matter.

133658.explanation: Agriculture and livestock farming are two different concepts and the municipality has no authority over this. But in principle, we are in favor of sustainable agriculture.

133659.explanation: The national government and province are the competent authorities for this topic. Buyout schemes are available for this issue.

133660.explanation: The national government and province are the competent authorities for this topic. They issue permits for these companies, not the municipality.

133661.explanation: Solar panels are an essential part of sustainable energy generation. Giving up agricultural land can be justified in the general interest.

133662.explanation: Local ownership is an important element in the success of the energy transition. Local initiatives, such as an energy cooperative, should be supported by the municipality by providing knowledge and proper channels for financing.

133663.explanation: People, including refugees, should not have to live on or from the streets. That is merciless.

133664.explanation: The dispersal law is the best way to get status holders out of the asylum seekers' center as quickly as possible, which benefits the AZC in Leersum. This barely increases the average waiting time for social rental housing.

133666.explanation: The heating guide provides good instructions on how to handle an open fireplace. Banning it in rural areas is also unenforceable. Citizens are well able to discuss and resolve common issues with each other.

133667.explanation: We need all sources of energy. Wind turbines make an important contribution to the energy challenge.

133668.explanation: We must learn to live together with wolves that exhibit normal behavior. The municipality must have sufficient tools (tracking collars, paintball). Shooting a wolf for which a shooting permit has been issued is also among these tools.

133669.explanation: Although it is not the municipality's responsibility that residents eat sustainably and locally, it is good if the municipality promotes this.

133670.explanation: We love the parade, the carriages, the Grietmarkt, and so on.

133671.explanation: Events have been organized in the past. Going back to zero events is not realistic. A new zoning plan is being worked on. That provides the framework for the area's development and thus also the possible uses.

133672.explanation: We adopted a policy vision on hospitality and terraces on September 19, 2024. Let's first evaluate that policy before coming up with new ideas.

133673.explanation: The current amount of advertising within the built-up area is not disturbing. Outside the built-up area, the province is the competent authority and has strict rules for this.

133674.explanation: Such a scheme already exists. The challenge is that the people for whom the scheme is intended also make use of it. Often shame plays a role in requesting help. And privacy legislation can also pose a barrier to reaching families who need support.

133675.explanation: It is important that young and old exercise; prevention is an important tool to promote good health. The government already encourages this, and we as a municipality also have a local policy for it.

133676.explanation: The municipality chose synthetic or natural grass in good consultation with the sports clubs. Maintenance costs are an important consideration. The installation and upkeep of fields weigh on the municipal budget.

133677.explanation: The library has an important social and informative function in all our villages, and that must remain so.

133678.explanation: The merging of two swimming pools (Driebergen and Doorn) into one new pool was proposed 7 years ago but was unfeasible at that time. Associations and residents massively protested, and the plan was withdrawn.

133679.explanation: The municipal council has previously cut back on playgrounds. Some playgrounds were sold off, and the municipality must ensure sufficient funds for the remaining playgrounds.

133680.explanation: Although Sunday is a day of rest for the Christian Union, we are not against subsidies for clubs that play sports on Sundays.

133681.explanation: Contact with animals is important for young and old: toddlers at a petting zoo get acquainted with animals, and elderly people with dementia get a stimulus to communicate through contact with animals.

133682.explanation: Camera surveillance is now used when necessary. Unfortunately, the use of cameras in today's society is increasingly necessary, but it is applied sparingly and for a limited period.

133683.explanation: The municipality has long prohibited new coffeeshops. This is recorded in the General Local Regulation. Drugs are harmful to society. They lead to increased crime and damage the environment. They also affect health.

133684.explanation: The municipality must create rescue plans for humans and animals, not only in case of fire but also other emergencies!

133685.explanation: The Utrechtse Heuvelrug municipality currently has six neighborhood officers. One officer per 5,000 residents would be more than 1.5 times the current number. Unfortunately, this is not very realistic given the current shortage of police officers.

133686.explanation: It is very good that minorities recognize themselves in authority figures, and indeed all professionals.

133687.explanation: The Christian Union is in favor of more enforcement officers (boa's), but there must be priority to allocate an additional budget for this.

133688.explanation: The municipality has limited territory and therefore little wildlife. The management of the forests is outsourced to terrain-managing organizations that implement their own policies in consultation with the Forestry Cooperatives (WBE's).

133691.explanation: Yes, converting existing buildings into residential use where possible offers opportunities for more homes. The province has created policy for this.

133692.explanation: The current target of 30% social housing is fine. There is a particular need for mid-rent housing. The target for that is 40%.

133693.explanation: The current residential care vision already allows a maximum of 6 floors, with the possibility to permit up to 9 floors in special cases.

133694.explanation: In the Utrechtse Heuvelrug municipality, there is an owner-occupancy obligation for certain (owner-occupied) homes through the introduction of purchase protection in the Housing Regulation, which means that the buyer must live there themselves, usually for a period of 4 years after purchase, to prevent speculation.

133695.explanation: This type of business generates a lot of traffic and takes up a lot of space. It is a kind of business activity we have not chosen in the Utrechtse Heuvelrug.

133696.explanation: The municipality has chosen to build as little surface area as possible in this location to create more space for green and nature. The small-scale homes mentioned in this statement are 'ground-bound' and require even more space.

133697.explanation: Until now, the municipality has followed national policy to prevent permanent habitation of recreational parks. Enforcement is applied with a human scale.

133698.explanation: During the past council period, we focused on infill locations within the built-up area. That is always tailor-made and requires a lot of time and energy. We need to build many homes in the coming period, so we also have to look for space outside the built-up area.

133699.explanation: Christian Union is not in favor of a maximum population of 52,000 and therefore not in favor of a building stop. The number of inhabitants does not say anything about having sufficient living space and privacy.

133700.explanation: During the past council period, we focused on infill locations within the built-up area. That is always tailor-made and requires a lot of time and energy. We need to build many homes in the coming period, so we also have to look for space outside the built-up area.

133701.explanation: Christian Union is in favor of temporary housing for informal care. However, permit issuance is necessary to maintain a good overview of additional buildings.

133702.explanation: The municipality currently applies 120% of the legal minimum. Depending on general economic developments (such as inflation), the municipality keeps a close eye on the situation.

133703.explanation: People are on social assistance for good reasons. Many social assistance recipients already do volunteer work. And if something is mandatory, it is not voluntary. Much more important is that people have personal conversations from time to time about their possibilities. That is current standard practice.

133704.explanation: The municipality already does its best to realize this in many ways.

133706.explanation: We need all parking spaces. In new neighborhoods, we can encourage people, for example, to make more use of shared cars.

133707.explanation: It is a good thing if cycling gets more space in traffic.
